macro_segments.title: Introduction to Hatarat Nedarim: Definition of Mumcheh and Criteria for Beit Din Members

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and transition to Daf 79; review of hatarat nedarim by yachid mumcheh or three
Debate on the definition of mumcheh: semicha from Moshe Rabbeinu versus high knowledge versus rashei hamatot
Shulchan Aruch's position on mumcheh as expertise in halacha; modern applicability
Criteria for Beit Din members: requirement for gimur vesavir versus savir alone
Shulchan Aruch on savir standard for all three members of Beit Din

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Chanina's Theory of Silence as Hakeimah: Intent-Based Reinterpretation of the Pasuk

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chanina's radical interpretation: silence becomes hakeimah only when silence indicates acceptance of the neder
Alternative scenarios: silence to spite or manipulate the wife does not constitute hakeimah
Rabbi Chanina's conclusion: even after ten days, heifer is possible if silence is not for acceptance
Rava's challenge: examination of when father regains reshut after husband's silence or heifer
Rava's logic: any silence after 24 hours seems to be hakeimah regardless of intent
Gemara's response: distinguishing between shatak on purpose to make kiyum versus shatak stam (neutral silence)
Ran's explanation: two scenarios—immediate kiyum by action versus kiyum contemplation requiring 24 hours

macro_segments.title: Comparative Strength of Hakeimah versus Heifer: Mechanisms and Reversibility

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's challenge: examples of hakeimah stronger than heifer and vice versa
Hakeimah does not require verbalization; silence works after 24 hours
Heifer always requires verbalization; silence never constitutes heifer
Kiyum does not require verbal statement; can be done in one's heart
Symmetry: once kiyum is done, heifer cannot be done; once heifer is done, kiyum cannot be done
Rabbi Yochanan: hakeimah can be undone through hashrat nedarim with a chakham; heifer cannot be undone

macro_segments.title: Tanaic Challenges to Rabbi Chanina: Three Tyufot Rejecting Intent-Based Limitation

micro_segments.title: First tyufa: Baraita explicitly states silence for spite is still hakeimah by end of day
Gemara resolves: the word 'silence' appears three times in pasuk, teaching three cases including spite
Second tyufa: Rava's argument from Shabbat heter—if heifer forbidden on Shabbat, silence would be legitimate
Rava's inference: necessity to permit heifer on Shabbat proves silence becomes hakeimah regardless of reason
Third tyufa: Rav Ashi on ignorance of right to meifer—silence still becomes hakeimah
Distinction between Rabbi Meir and Chakhamim on when hearing of neder occurs halachically

macro_segments.title: Ran's Explanation of Non-Verbal Kiyum and Rabbi Akiva Eiger's Challenge on Evidence

micro_segments.title: Ran: silence until end of day proves acceptance without verbalization; shows difference from heifer
Ran's logic: must wait till end of day because intent is not evident until then
Ran: if one actively desires kiyum, it works immediately without waiting; it's like crossing the finish line
Rabbi Akiva Eiger's challenge: if we rely on what you're thinking but normally don't have access to thoughts, how can immediate kiyum work?
Gilyon HaShas: internal thoughts only matter if evident to others; 24-hour silence is evidence
Gilyon HaShas resolution: immediate kiyum would require observable evidence like thumbs up, not mere internal thought

macro_segments.title: Categories of Nedarim Subject to Heifer: Inuy Nefesh and Bein Ish Leishah

micro_segments.title: Opening statement: these are the nedarim that the husband can meifer
First category: nedarim with inuy nefesh—vows that afflict the body/soul
Examples of inuy nefesh: not bathing, not putting on makeup
Second category referenced: bein ish leishah—vows affecting the marital relationship
Rabbi Yosi's position: bathing and makeup are not inuy nefesh; only complete abstention is inuy nefesh
Examples of non-inuy nefesh: forbidding fruit from one country (can get from another); forbidding one store's produce (can use alternate store)
Exception: if storekeeper is the sole practical supplier due to credit, then forbidding him is inuy nefesh

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Analysis: Hakeimah Permanence versus Bein Ish Leishah Reversibility Upon Remarriage

micro_segments.title: Gemara: only inuy nefesh nedarim are mufar; non-inuy nefesh are not mufar
Citation of Baraita: both inuy nefesh and bein ish leishah can be meifer
Question: if both categories can be meifer, why does Rabbi Yosi reject bathing as inuy nefesh?
Theory presented: inuy nefesh nedarim work forever when meifer; bein ish leishah only while married
Case of Rabbi Yochanan ben Nuri: husband should meifer wife's vow forbidding her labor lest she remarries and vow takes effect
Gemara rejects theory: even bein ish leishah remains meifer after divorce
Refined distinction: inuy nefesh meifer for himself and for others (after remarriage); bein ish leishah meifer for himself only, not for new husband
